Each lesson was clearly designed to be taught at a different point in the school year as part of a connected curriculum series.

deck.whats_the_same: Both lessons teach the same activity — students learn about community garden history in New York City and make seed bursts (balls of clay, compost, and wildflower seeds) to plant in their neighborhoods. They share the same summary, objectives, materials list, and basic lesson flow.
deck.whats_different: Lesson A is written for 3rd and 4th graders in summer and includes richer discussion prompts, a reference to walking field trips, a word search extension, and class norm-setting. Lesson B is written for kindergartners and 1st graders in spring, uses simpler language with guided picture-based storytelling about the 1970s, and has fewer extension activities to match younger learners.

deck.whats_the_same: All three lessons teach the same core idea — that seeds travel by floating on water, blowing in the wind, or hitching a ride on animals — and all have students build seed models from craft materials to explore these methods hands-on.
deck.whats_different: Lesson B is written for 7th and 8th graders, uses four challenge stations (including an 'exploding jungle'), and has more complex setup and prep instructions; Lessons A and C are both aimed at younger students (grades 2–3), but Lesson A is a two-day plan that also takes students outside to actually plant seeds and includes formal academic standards, while Lesson C is a simpler one-day indoor lesson with a more streamlined format and no listed standards.

deck.whats_the_same: Both lessons are the same garden orientation activity where students use their five senses to explore the garden, follow the same 45-minute class flow (welcome and sense gestures, scavenger hunt, group sharing and tasting), use the same materials, and teach the same garden safety rules.
deck.whats_different: Lesson B is a more developed version: it adds explicit instructions for 3K and Pre-K students (a guided walk instead of the worksheet), includes a required back-pocket activity, and has more complete teaching notes such as a Garden Connection section and a Critical Thinking section. Lesson A is simpler and has a written summary, but is otherwise an earlier, less complete draft of the same lesson.
deck.survivor_why: Lesson B is the more recent and more fully developed version, with explicit grade-level differentiation for 3K/Pre-K students and a required back-pocket activity that Lesson A lacks.

deck.whats_the_same: Both lessons are the same September sixth-grade garden class: students use a map to identify summer crops, water the garden on day two, and do a tasting at the end of day one. The standards, materials, vocabulary, watering instructions, and closing extension activity are identical.
deck.whats_different: Lesson B weaves a year-long pizza garden project throughout the whole lesson — the opening introduces students to the pizza project, the map activity asks them to flag plants that might taste good on a pizza, and the closing circle asks them to share what they found for the pizza. Lesson A is a general summer garden exploration with no pizza framing built into the activities (it only mentions the pizza project briefly at the very end as a preview of next time). A teacher running the pizza curriculum would use Lesson B; a teacher doing a standalone garden orientation would use Lesson A.

deck.whats_the_same: Both lessons cover the exact same topic and sequence: students learn about fungi, make a sourdough starter together, then assemble pasta bowls with mushrooms and vegetables. The materials, timing, closing ritual, and most of the wording are nearly identical.
deck.whats_different: The key difference is the grade level each lesson is written for. The Kindergarten version uses simple language ('suuuper tiny living things'), has the instructor cook at the front and read a story aloud to students, and keeps student tasks basic. The Middle School version uses scientific vocabulary ('unicellular fungi'), has student teams cooking at their own tables, and distributes recipe cards for students to follow independently — more autonomy and complexity throughout.

deck.whats_the_same: Both lessons teach the same dish — a mushroom 'chicken' over yellow rice inspired by NYC halal carts — and share the same core story about street vending history, the same food-justice themes, the same back-pocket worksheet activity, and the same PS 7 school site.
deck.whats_different: Lesson A is written specifically for grades 6–8 and includes the full, detailed recipes right in the lesson text. Lesson B is a multi-grade version spanning kindergarten all the way through 8th grade, with three separate opening activities tailored to K–3, grades 4–5, and grades 6–8 respectively; it also references printed task cards for younger students and adds tomatoes and lettuce as ingredients for the elementary version. Lesson B is the more recently added and more broadly adapted version, but it lacks a written summary and some of the recipe detail that Lesson A carries.

deck.whats_the_same: All three lessons teach the same sun printing art activity, where students walk through the garden collecting leaves, sticks, and plant parts, then arrange them on paper and use sunlight to create an image. The step-by-step script, opening questions, and closing ritual are nearly word-for-word the same across all three.
deck.whats_different: Lessons A and B are both written for kindergarten and first grade and are essentially the same document saved twice — Lesson A has a written summary and a 'Year A' label while Lesson B lacks a summary but adds 'relationship skills' as a learning goal. Lesson C is a real adaptation for fourth and fifth graders: it asks students to think about cultural connections to art, frames the social-emotional goals differently (emphasizing self-management and collaboration), and includes a prompt about critical thinking and independence that the younger-grade versions do not have. Lessons A and B are redundant copies of each other and one should be retired.

deck.whats_the_same: Both lessons teach the exact same cooking activity: students explore root vegetables, compare them to a strawberry, then cut carrots and make a sauce — roasting half the roots and serving the other half raw so students can compare the two side by side. The opening discussion, core tasks, and grade range are identical.
deck.whats_different: Lesson A is a clean, fully written-out lesson plan with complete objectives and a clear summary. Lesson B appears to be an earlier or parallel draft of the same lesson — it has more sections (a vocabulary list, extension activity, detailed supplies list, and tray setup guide) but several of those sections contain placeholder text like 'Students will learn to… and discuss…' and 'See appendix A for lesson objectives,' meaning it was never fully finished. Both plans teach the same activity in the same way.
deck.survivor_why: Lesson A is fully written with no placeholder text, complete objectives, and a polished summary, making it the ready-to-use version of this lesson.

deck.whats_the_same: Both lessons are the exact same Apple Story activity for Kindergarten and 1st grade — students taste apple chips, listen to a story about where apples come from, act it out, color and assemble a class book, and share what they learned. The materials, prep steps, extension activities, and closing ritual are all identical.
deck.whats_different: Lesson A is more completely filled out: it has a written summary, more themes and competencies listed, a cooking skills note, and the copyright line at the end. Lesson B adds one small step at the very start of class — asking students to show with their thumbs how they are feeling — and its text is formatted in a table style rather than plain paragraphs, suggesting it came from a different document version.
deck.survivor_why: Lesson A has a written summary, richer tags and competencies, a cooking skills field, and the copyright attribution, making it the more complete record.

deck.whats_the_same: Most of these lessons are for the youngest students (Pre-K and 3K) and all take place in or around the school garden, asking children to look for signs of a season, explore plant parts with their senses, and celebrate what they find with a tasting and a closing circle.
deck.whats_different: The set is a mix of genuinely different things: Lessons C, D, E, and F are four separate seasonal lessons (Winter, Fall, Summer, Spring) for Pre-K — each one stands on its own. Lessons G and H are nearly identical versions of the same 'I spy plant parts on trays' lesson, with G being more complete (it includes a Canva materials link and a series note); H should be retired. Lessons D and I are also near-identical copies of the same Fall lesson for Pre-K, with D being more complete; I should be retired. Lessons A and B do not belong to this family at all — A is a 6th-grade summer garden map activity, and B is a 3rd-grade pesticide food-chain game.

deck.whats_the_same: All three lessons are about the water cycle and are meant for indoor settings in fall or winter, and they all follow the same general lesson plan format from the same organization.
deck.whats_different: Each lesson does something completely different: Lesson A has students build their own miniature rainforest terrariums to watch the water cycle happen in a jar, which works for a wide range of grades K–8. Lesson B is a cooking and art class for the youngest students (preschool through 2nd grade) where kids make banana snowmen on skewers and seed mosaics to celebrate winter and snow. Lesson C takes 2nd graders outside to observe how water behaves differently on pavement versus in a garden, then plays a physical acting game where students wear water cycle step necklaces and pass a raindrop around, followed by actually watering the garden.

deck.whats_the_same: Both lessons are the exact same Three Sisters seed study for grades 4-5 in the fall, covering companion planting, Indigenous heritage month, and using the same opening story, discussion questions, seed-sorting activity, and closing ritual word for word.
deck.whats_different: Lesson B is a more developed version: it adds a second activity variation where students practice a planting pattern on paper plates, includes a garden visit to see the Three Sisters bed, offers additional back-pocket resources like a cooking video featuring a Navajo chef, and carries more tags. Lesson A is simpler and has a written summary, but is otherwise a shorter earlier draft of the same plan.
deck.survivor_why: Lesson B is the more complete and more recently added version, offering two activity variations, a garden visit component, and richer back-pocket resources.

deck.whats_the_same: Both lessons teach students about the Three Sisters — corn, beans, and squash — their role in Native American and Latin American food traditions, and how the three plants support each other in the garden. Both are indoor cooking lessons that use the same core ingredients to make a Latin American dish with a sauce on the side.
deck.whats_different: The two lessons make completely different foods: one makes enchiladas (a Mexican dish with a chipotle-tomato sauce, taught in the fall) and the other makes empanadas (a stuffed pastry with chimichurri sauce, taught in the spring). The enchilada lesson runs students through rotating stations and includes grade-specific writing and math activities for grades K–5, while the empanada lesson follows a single whole-class cooking flow and serves a wider range of grades from pre-K through 8th grade with its own detailed recipe and supplies list.

deck.whats_the_same: Both lessons teach the same Lenape Three Sisters puppet show to pre-K and 3K students, using the same puppet characters, the same discussion questions about corn, beans, and squash, the same hands-on tasting and drawing activity, and the same materials list.
deck.whats_different: Lesson B is a more developed version of the same lesson — it adds an opening ritual (thumb check), a formal closing ritual with specific teacher prompts, a garden connection section, a critical-thinking reflection, more tags, and notes the location as both indoor and outdoor rather than indoor only. Lesson A has a written summary that Lesson B is missing, but otherwise Lesson B is the fuller, more polished version.
deck.survivor_why: Lesson B is the more complete plan, with an opening ritual, closing ritual, critical-thinking prompts, garden connection section, and broader tagging, making it more useful for instructors.

deck.whats_the_same: Both lessons teach the exact same dish — Tortilla Española — to elementary students in the spring, using eggs from the school's garden chickens. They share the same three-station rotation: one table for chopping vegetables, one for egg cracking and whisking, and one academic table with differentiated activities for younger students (eggs around the world) and older students (life cycle of a chicken).
deck.whats_different: Lesson A is a much longer, more fully developed lesson plan — it includes both a single-period and double-period schedule, specific academic standards, detailed step-by-step instructions for each station, a final trivia game or read-aloud activity, appendix references, and covers Pre-K through 5th grade. Lesson B covers the same content but is written in a shorter, streamlined template with an opening ritual / closing ritual structure, a built-in materials list, and a few extra social-emotional learning tags — it reads more like a quick reference version of the same lesson.
deck.survivor_why: Lesson A is far more complete, with double and single period plans, academic standards, Pre-K inclusion, detailed station instructions, and supporting appendices that give teachers more to work with.

deck.whats_the_same: Both lessons teach the exact same Varenyky cooking activity: students explore Ukrainian culture using a world map, then work at three stations to make dough, assemble dumplings, and prepare the potato-mushroom filling, finishing with a shared meal. The objectives, station names, back-pocket activities, materials list, prep notes, and anticipated problems are identical.
deck.whats_different: The two lessons are the same lesson saved in two different template formats. Lesson A uses a clean, prose-style layout and has a fuller set of tags and skills recorded. Lesson B uses an older fill-in-the-table template and includes a couple of extra prompt fields (Garden Connection, Critical Thinking) that are mostly left blank, but its metadata tagging is thinner and it has no written summary.
deck.survivor_why: Lesson A has a written summary, richer metadata (more skills, ingredients, SEL, and cultural responsiveness tags), and cleaner formatting, making it the more complete and usable record.

deck.whats_the_same: Three of the five lessons teach the same Vegetable Ramen cooking activity, and two teach a Food Waste lesson — all five are school kitchen lessons set in winter that share themes of healthy eating, environmental awareness, and cooking skills.
deck.whats_different: Lesson D is nearly word-for-word the same as Lesson A (same Green Room introduction, same full step list with miso, corn, broccoli, and furikake seasoning) but has no written summary and less complete information, making it a redundant copy of A that should be retired. Lesson C is a genuinely distinct version of the Ramen lesson that strips out some cooking steps and adds an entirely different garden activity — planting and growing pea shoots — so it stands on its own. Lessons B and E both cover food waste but differ meaningfully: Lesson E adds a craft project (a food scrap collage) and a garden connection section that Lesson B does not include, so both are worth keeping.

deck.whats_the_same: Both lessons cover exactly the same introductory garden class: students learn garden rules, match garden jobs to their definitions, rotate through hands-on stations in the school garden, share a tasting together, and have the option to write a haiku as a back-pocket activity. The objectives, materials, prep steps, and nearly all wording are identical.
deck.whats_different: Lesson A is clearly written out for 6th grade in the fall and includes a short summary, while Lesson B has no summary written and extends the grade range to include 7th grade and adds summer as a season — but the actual lesson content never changes to reflect those additions; the grade-level and site-variation sections are left blank. Lesson B also uses a more template-like layout with extra empty fields, making it feel like a work-in-progress copy of the same lesson.
deck.survivor_why: Lesson A has a written summary, a polished narrative format, and is the more complete, ready-to-use version of the two.

deck.whats_the_same: All four lessons are part of the same early-childhood garden series for 3K and Pre-K students. Two teach what a gardener wears (using a paper dress-up craft), and two teach what a gardener does (using a cover-crop planting activity). They share the same structure: a welcome, a main activity, and a closing song.
deck.whats_different: The set actually contains two genuinely different lessons — one about gardener clothing and one about gardening jobs — making them parts of a series, not copies of each other. However, within each topic there is a duplicate: the 'gardener wear' lessons (A and C) are nearly identical, with C being a slightly less complete version of A; likewise, the 'gardener do' lessons (B and D) are nearly identical, with D covering only 3K while B covers both 3K and PK and has a fuller written summary. The team should retire C (keeping A) and retire D (keeping B).

deck.whats_the_same: Both lessons cover the exact same activity for 3K and PK students: a fall garden tour followed by a read-aloud or puppet show about garden animals, a hands-on food processing task (like shucking beans), and a closing tasting. The agenda, all three activity options, the safety rules, the materials list, and the back-pocket backup activity are word-for-word the same.
deck.whats_different: The lessons are the same lesson saved twice in two different document formats — Lesson A is laid out as plain paragraphs while Lesson B uses a table-based template. Lesson A includes a written summary and the 'Year A' label; Lesson B has no written summary but lists a few extra tags (like Arts as a subject and Self-awareness as an SEL skill). There are no meaningful differences in what students actually do.
deck.survivor_why: Lesson A has a written summary and cleaner, more readable formatting, making it easier for instructors to pick up and use quickly.

deck.whats_the_same: Both lessons teach students about the difference between whole grains and processed grains, and both have students make granola together as the hands-on cooking activity. They share the same background explanation about the seed's endosperm and the same cooking skills focus.
deck.whats_different: Lesson A is written for middle schoolers (grades 6–8) and keeps things straightforward — a group discussion, making granola, and a brief reflection. Lesson B is for kindergarten through 2nd grade and is much more detailed, with grade-by-grade instructions, a holiday gift-giving component (students bag the granola as presents), a bread-and-orange-juice comparison activity, a worksheet, a coloring sheet, and specific SEL work around gratitude — making it a significantly richer and differently shaped lesson.

deck.whats_the_same: Both lessons teach the same winter fruit salad activity — students learn about seasonality, read (or listen to) 'An Orange in January,' and chop fruit together to make and taste a salad. The core recipe, cooking skills, and learning goals are nearly identical.
deck.whats_different: Lesson B is written specifically for a mobile educator who travels from school to school and needs to introduce themselves, set class expectations, and work with any grade from pre-K through 8th grade using optional, flexible steps. Lesson A is for a resident teacher who already knows the students and uses fixed, grade-differentiated activities (separate instructions for K–2 versus 3–5). The ingredient lists also differ slightly — Lesson B adds kiwi and pomegranate seeds — and Lesson B has a distinct closing ritual ('Thank you gardeners, Thank you Cooks') that reflects its traveling-classroom roots.

deck.whats_the_same: Both lessons teach the exact same activity: students follow a simple recipe to mix compostable garden scraps into a 'worm breakfast,' learning what goes in a compost bin while practicing measuring and taking turns. The step-by-step class flow, materials list, grade-level variations, back-pocket activity, and closing ritual are all identical.
deck.whats_different: The two lessons are the same lesson saved twice at different times. Lesson B was added later and carries slightly more metadata tags (a season label, broader grade range listed, and extra social-emotional skills), but its lesson text is poorly formatted with leftover table symbols from a document import, and it has no written summary. Lesson A has cleaner, more readable text and a proper summary paragraph.
deck.survivor_why: Lesson A has clean, readable lesson text and a written summary, while Lesson B's text is garbled with table-import artifacts and is missing a summary.

deck.whats_the_same: Both lessons are the exact same Worm Study lesson for early childhood grades — they share the same objectives, the worm dance, the small-group observation activity, the drawing and writing tasks, the materials list, the closing ritual, and the back pocket activities word for word.
deck.whats_different: The content is the same lesson saved twice. Lesson A is the cleaner, more complete copy: it has a written summary, a copyright notice, cleaner formatting, and richer theme tags. Lesson B appears to be a later import that was pulled in from a table-based document, leaving '[Table]' markers throughout the text, no written summary, and slightly less complete metadata.
deck.survivor_why: Lesson A has a written summary, cleaner formatting, a copyright notice, and more complete metadata tags, making it the more polished and usable copy.
